impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder that conditions patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products engages in unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the defense that tied products are 'nonstaple goods' lacking substantial non-infringing uses shields the patent holder from antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act for conditioning licenses to its fuel-vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the jury's finding that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses beyond the patent, defeating Ingevity's 'nonstaple goods' defense. The decision affirms that patent holders cannot leverage patent rights to foreclose competition in adjacent unpatented product markets.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses facing anticompetitive tying arrangements by patent holders gain a clear precedent that conditioning patent licenses on purchase of unpatented products constitutes actionable antitrust conduct when the tied product has substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who bundle patent licenses with product purchase requirements face significant antitrust exposure. Companies using licensing strategies that tie patented and unpatented products must carefully evaluate whether tied products have substantial non-infringing uses.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in obtaining patents and on a Walker Process fraud claim for attempted monopolization based on procurement of patents through fraud on the patent office.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct (which had been granted against Tenaris) and Walker Process fraud (which had been granted against Global Tubing), finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The case involves allegations that Tenaris failed to disclose prior art (the CYMAX Documents) during patent prosecution for its coiled tubing patents. The decision sends both the patent enforceability and antitrust claims back for trial.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers benefit from the court's willingness to allow Walker Process antitrust claims to proceed to trial where there is evidence of potential fraud on the patent office, providing a pathway to challenge monopolistic conduct based on fraudulently obtained patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face continued litigation risk on both inequitable conduct and antitrust fronts. Companies acquiring prior art through asset purchases must ensure thorough disclosure during patent prosecution or risk both patent unenforceability and antitrust liability.

impacts.caseName: Seagen Inc. v. Daiichi Sankyo Company, Ltd.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the asserted patent claims covering antibody-drug conjugates with tetrapeptide linkers were invalid for lack of written description and enablement where the specification disclosed only dipeptide linkers.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed a $41+ million jury verdict in favor of Seagen, holding that the asserted claims of the '039 patent were invalid for lack of written description and enablement. The patent claimed ADCs with tetrapeptide linker units, but the specification only described dipeptide linkers. The court found that the specification did not demonstrate the inventor possessed the full scope of the claimed invention, particularly the tetrapeptide variants. This is a significant ruling in the pharmaceutical/biotech patent space regarding the scope of genus claims.
impacts.positiveImplications: Pharmaceutical and biotech companies accused of infringing broadly-claimed patents gain a strong precedent for challenging patent validity where specifications disclose only a narrow subset of the claimed genus.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the life sciences industry face heightened written description and enablement scrutiny for claims that extend beyond the specific embodiments disclosed in the specification. Broad genus claims covering therapeutic molecules are at increased risk of invalidation.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods and systems for a server to tailor content delivery specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed a jury verdict finding Netflix liable for patent infringement, holding that the asserted patent claims directed to server-side tailoring of media content specifications to a wireless device's capabilities are patent-ineligible under § 101. The court found the claims directed to abstract ideas. This decision required reversal of the entire judgment in favor of Netflix, eliminating a $2.5 million damages award.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies facing patent infringement claims related to content delivery and device-adaptive streaming gain a strong defensive precedent under § 101 for invalidating claims directed to conventional server-side content adaptation.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with portfolios covering content delivery optimization and adaptive streaming technologies face increased vulnerability to § 101 challenges, particularly where claims recite functional results without sufficiently specific technical implementations.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities for asynchronous viewing of data streams state patent-eligible subject matter under 35 U.S.C. § 101, as evaluated on a motion to dismiss.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal of a patent infringement complaint against Google for failure to state a claim, finding the asserted web conferencing patent claims ineligible under § 101. The claims covered systems enabling participants to view web conference sessions in real-time, delayed, or after completion at different playback rates. The court's willingness to resolve § 101 at the pleading stage reinforces that patent eligibility can be determined early in litigation.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies can secure early dismissal of patent infringement suits involving claims directed to abstract concepts like web conferencing with time-shifting, reducing litigation costs significantly.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent assertion entities and patent holders face the risk of early dismissal under § 101 at the 12(b)(6) stage, limiting their ability to extract settlements through prolonged litigation.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Patent Trial and Appeal Board correctly construed the claim term 'encryption information' in a patent directed to streaming partially encrypted media content, and whether the claims were rendered obvious under the correct construction.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the PTAB's claim construction of the term 'encryption information' in DivX's patent on streaming partially encrypted media content, vacated the Board's decision rejecting Netflix's obviousness challenge, and remanded for further proceedings. The decision impacts DRM and adaptive bitrate streaming technologies widely used in the entertainment and technology industries.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming service providers and technology companies benefit from a more restrictive reading of DRM-related patent claims, potentially narrowing the scope of enforceable patents covering encrypted media streaming.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the digital media streaming and encryption space face the risk that their claims may be construed more narrowly and found obvious on remand.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Patent Trial and Appeal Board governing the exercise of discretionary authority to decline institution of inter partes reviews constitute legislative rules requiring notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed, holding that the PTO Director's instructions governing discretionary IPR non-institution decisions are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under § 553(b). The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, the instructions do not bind the Director, and non-institution decisions are insulated from judicial review. This preserves the PTO's broad discretion to decline IPR petitions.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's retained broad discretion to deny IPR institution, reducing the risk that their patents will be subjected to inter partes review challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies relying on IPR as a cost-effective mechanism to challenge patent validity face continued uncertainty, as the PTO Director retains unfettered discretion to deny institution without the procedural safeguards of notice-and-comment rulemaking.

impacts.caseName: Game Plan, Inc. v. Uninterrupted IP, LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Trademark Trial and Appeal Board correctly cancelled Game Plan's registration for its 'I AM MORE THAN AN ATHLETE' mark and dismissed Game Plan's opposition to Uninterrupted IP's marks based on acquired common law rights through an asset purchase agreement.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the TTAB's cancellation of Game Plan's trademark registration and dismissal of its opposition to Uninterrupted IP's marks containing 'MORE THAN AN ATHLETE.' The decision turned on the validity of common law trademark rights acquired through an asset purchase agreement that transferred both the mark and associated goodwill. The ruling reinforces that trademark rights can be effectively transferred through properly structured asset purchases and used to establish priority in opposition proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies acquiring trademark rights through asset purchases gain confirmation that properly structured assignments transferring both the mark and associated goodwill can establish priority in TTAB proceedings.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trademark registrants who fail to present evidence at trial risk cancellation of their registrations, and may lose priority disputes to parties who acquire earlier common law rights through asset purchases.

impacts.caseName: Coda Development s.r.o. v. Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether Goodyear was entitled to judgment as a matter of law overturning a jury verdict of trade secret misappropriation and $64 million in damages related to self-inflating tire technology.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed JMOL overturning a $64 million jury verdict ($2.8M compensatory + $61.2M punitive) for trade secret misappropriation related to self-inflating tire technology. The court also affirmed denial of correction of inventorship. The decision addresses the evidentiary standards for establishing trade secret misappropriation, particularly in complex technology collaboration contexts.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies engaged in technology collaborations and partnerships benefit from the court's rigorous scrutiny of trade secret misappropriation claims, requiring plaintiffs to clearly establish that information qualifies as a trade secret and was actually misappropriated.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trade secret owners face a high bar in establishing misappropriation claims, particularly where the alleged secrets relate to technology shared during collaborative development relationships. Even large jury verdicts may be overturned on JMOL.

impacts.caseName: Wonderland Switzerland AG v. Evenflo Company, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly entered a permanent injunction covering both asserted patents after the jury found infringement, and whether the jury's findings of infringement under the doctrine of equivalents were supported.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed in part, reversed in part, and vacated in part in a patent infringement case involving children's car seat designs. The court addressed the scope of permanent injunctions in patent cases and the application of the doctrine of equivalents. The decision impacts product design freedom for consumer goods manufacturers in a significant product safety market.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in consumer product markets receive guidance on obtaining injunctive relief following jury verdicts of infringement, including the potential for broad injunctions covering multiple asserted patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Consumer product manufacturers face continued risk of permanent injunctions that may extend beyond the specific products found to infringe, potentially requiring product redesigns across product lines.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses in countervailing duty and antidumping investigations as confidential is consistent with the governing statute and whether 19 U.S.C. § 1516a abrogates the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's holding that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the governing statute, and that § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial records. The decision requires the ITC to apply confidentiality designations on a case-by-case basis consistent with statutory criteria, increasing transparency in trade remedy proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and parties in trade remedy proceedings benefit from increased transparency, as the ITC can no longer automatically shield all questionnaire responses from public access. This may allow better insight into competitors' trade practices and government decision-making.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting commercially sensitive information in ITC questionnaire responses face greater risk of public disclosure. Businesses that previously relied on blanket confidentiality must now justify confidential treatment on a case-by-case basis.

impacts.caseName: Secretary of Defense v. Pratt & Whitney
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an agreement between Pratt & Whitney and the government regarding the inclusion of 'Drag' costs in the overhead pool for cost-plus government contracts was valid, and whether the Board's decision on the overhead allocation base was a final appealable decision.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit addressed cost allocation disputes in cost-plus government defense contracts involving Pratt & Whitney's aircraft engine business. The court dismissed the appeal regarding the overhead allocation base for lack of finality but reversed the Board's finding of an enforceable agreement on inclusion of 'Drag' costs in the overhead pool, holding the agreement invalid. The decision has significant implications for defense contractors using collaboration agreements with parts suppliers.
impacts.positiveImplications: The government benefits from invalidation of the cost agreement, potentially reducing overpayments to defense contractors who use unconventional supplier collaboration arrangements to allocate costs to government contracts.
impacts.negativeImplications: Defense contractors using collaboration agreements and revenue-sharing arrangements with suppliers face increased scrutiny of how those costs are allocated to government cost-plus contracts, potentially requiring restructuring of supplier relationships.

impacts.caseName: In re Bayou Grande Coffee Roasting Co.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the TTAB erred in refusing to register the mark KAHWA for cafés and coffee shops under the doctrine of foreign equivalents, and whether the Board's genericness and descriptiveness findings based on an alternative meaning constituted impermissible new grounds of refusal.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the TTAB's refusal to register the mark KAHWA for cafés and coffee shops. The court addressed the doctrine of foreign equivalents and the Board's authority to raise new grounds of refusal based on alternative meanings of foreign-language marks. The decision provides important guidance on trademark registration of foreign-language terms and the limits of the PTO's ability to shift grounds during the appellate process.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses seeking to register foreign-language marks benefit from limitations on the PTO's ability to change the basis for refusal on appeal and from the court's scrutiny of the doctrine of foreign equivalents as applied to marks with multiple meanings.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the OPM regulation requiring written authorization for overtime (5 C.F.R. § 550.111(c)) is a valid exercise of rulemaking authority under the statute requiring payment for overtime 'officially ordered or approved' (5 U.S.C. § 5542(a)).
impacts.description: Sitting en banc, the Federal Circuit upheld OPM's longstanding regulation requiring written authorization for federal employee overtime claims, affirming dismissal of Count II of a class action by IHS nurses who worked extensive unpaid overtime during the COVID-19 pandemic. However, on panel remand, the court reversed dismissal of claims under other compensation statutes (38 U.S.C. § 7453 and regulations for nighttime, Sunday, and holiday pay), allowing those claims to proceed. The decision resolves an 80-year-old regulatory framework question while opening the door for other pay claims.
impacts.positiveImplications: Federal agencies benefit from the validation of the written overtime authorization requirement, providing a clear administrative framework for managing overtime liability.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal healthcare employers, particularly IHS, face potential liability for unpaid nighttime, Sunday, holiday, and miscellaneous compensation enhancements. The reversal on Counts I and III-V signals that agencies cannot deny all categories of enhanced pay to Title 5 healthcare employees eligible for Title 38 compensation benefits.

impacts.caseName: Adnexus Inc. v. Meta Platforms, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court erred in dismissing a patent infringement complaint for failure to state a claim where the plaintiff attached claim charts and preliminary infringement contentions to the amended complaint.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated the dismissal of Adnexus's patent infringement complaint against Meta's Lead Ads product and remanded for further proceedings. The case addresses the sufficiency of patent infringement pleadings at the 12(b)(6) stage, particularly when plaintiffs attach detailed claim charts and infringement contentions. The decision provides guidance on the pleading threshold for patent infringement claims against major technology companies.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the court's recognition that detailed claim charts and infringement contentions appended to a complaint may satisfy pleading requirements, making it harder for defendants to obtain early dismissals.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies face reduced ability to obtain early dismissal of patent infringement suits at the pleading stage when plaintiffs provide claim charts mapping patent limitations to accused products.
